speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 4th, 1995. The survivor is Anna Goldenberg-Schwarcz. And this is tape number 4.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with the Goldenberg family for over a year. They welcomed me. They shared their meager piece of bread with me. I must say, they gave me back my life. They restored in me the faith that there are some people, still, that care, that they are ready to share whatever they have. I felt very uncomfortable taking that meager piece of bread, but they gave it to me generously. And I had no choice. I had to accept it. Now, they also took in, later, two young boys that they had the same, similar story that mine, that also wandered around without-- without a permanent place. And they would have been killed also. They had asked the Kulisz family, from the Kucer's shelter, they should take in one, and they will take in one. Because the Goldenbergs had a very small shelter. So Kucer-- Kulisz said, again, I am determined to save my family. I cannot be generous to anybody. They took in those two boys, the Goldenbergs, and asked Schindelova, maybe, to make a bigger bunker, because there was absolutely no room for 10 people. Her brother came and dug another shelter. It was a little bigger, so that we were able to say there, all 10 of us. And they shared. They were seven from their family. You know, we were three total strangers. They shared whatever they had with us. Life in the bunker is no picnic. But we felt that we are with a family. We are not alone, which meant-- meant the world to us-- we were with our fellow Jews-- that they care. And our destiny would have been whatever their is. Fear did not leave us for a second, because the German army went run further and further into Russia. And there was almost no hope. But they, too, were very optimistic. And we all sat there, hoping.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Schindelovas shared their food with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, they were also in a better position, the Goldenbergs, just before they left the ghetto. Being that they were out of the ghetto, they dispersed some or-- some of their possessions to other Gentiles. And they used to send Schindelova's brother to get it for them. So any possession that they got and returned, they sold for food. And that's how she was able to provide for all 10 of us. Schindelova lived with a sister, with a invalid sister, Vera Kucerowa. And the two of them kept home. And they provided for all 10 of us. Now, we were fairly clean. At night, we were washing whatever we had, and kept ourselves clean, and attended to our daily necessities.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the shelter look like, physically, if you can remember it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Physically, it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't point to anything here. Tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, maybe, as big as a-- as a couch. I cannot give you the-- the dimensions, but we were able to sleep-- to sleep, because they put board in it. So some people slept up on the-- up, like bunk beds, let's put it that, on top of some of the-- One of the boys was snoring. And that was a very dangerous thing because, if somebody would come by, they would hear the snoring. So one of us wasn't sleeping. We tied up a rope to his leg. And every time he started to snore, we'd pulled the-- the-- the rope, and he got up [INAUDIBLE]. They also had a dog. [? Warshak ?] was the name of the dog. So that dog, when he heard or felt danger, he started to bark. And then we knew that we have to be quiet and-- and sit there, wait-- wait for the unknown.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the dimensions of the room?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I cannot tell you that. A-- like a very, very tiny room. And it was dark there, always dark. The earth was soft when we moved there-- when we got in. And after a year being there, the Earth was very, very hard. We breathed in all that moisture. But somehow, we were healthy. We were not sick through that time. My mother-in-law had gallstones. She never suffered with the gall stones. Schindelova gave us whatever she was able to get, sometimes even a chicken, or meat. My mother-in-law never ate the chicken, because she was very religious. She didn't want to eat non-kosher. But we did, because we felt that we have to sustain ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there heat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't-- there wasn't-- it wasn't cold. It was down in the cellar. It was not cold at all. It's very hard to describe the life there, but we made the best of it. And we were living with hope that, someday, we will be liberated. And some-- and that day arrived in February, 1944. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When the German army retreated, they stopped just there, where our-- with our bunker was. They put a machine gun there. They had telephones. They were like a communications station. They heard every voice. They gave the information. That time was the most crucial time for us, because we had to sit, not to breathe, even, the radio shouldn't detect our voices. She couldn't give us any food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our shelter, the engines was where the cows were standing. So she opened up-- once a day, she opened up the-- that latch, and she threw in some potatoes. So the Germans asked her, what-- why do you need so many potatoes? She says, her cows are used to eat potatoes. So she said-- she threw down, and we ate a potato a day. That went on for a few days, till the Germans retreated completely.

speaker: interviewee
text: The house caught fire. All of a sudden, we hear that Schindelova is screaming, fire! Fire! Fire! Then we knew we-- we had to get out, because we-- we were afraid that we will be there-- burned there. We emerged in the open world, the battleground between the Russians and the Germans. I saw in front of me dead Germans-- our side was still the Germans-- dead Germans, wild, wild soldiers, German soldiers, using the machine guns. We-- they-- they lost their head completely. They were not organized anymore. They were scattered, one and one, different places. It was not an army, but they were trying to fight their battle to the last. At night, the Russian army marched in. But that last episode was also our life was hanging on a-- on a string. Because when she said that the fire-- the-- the house took fire, and we were came out, we were afraid to stay outside. We were afraid for both-- for the-- for people that will recognize us and will tell them that those are Jews. And we were afraid that the battle was there, the fire.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we decided that maybe where the mother Kucherova is, on the other side of the road, maybe it's safer there. So first went Szloma's brother, Froiko and Asia. They crossed the-- they wanted to cross the road. They crossed it already. Then Szloma, and his mother, and I went. As we were approaching the-- the road, a German soldier stopped us and said, where are you going? You're prisoners. What-- who are you? Who are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, we took out the-- I took out my document, and I showed them the identification that I stole from that girl. And Szloma had, also, a document. We showed them that we are whoever we are. He said, I am going to take you to the-- to the high commander, German. And he determine what to do with you. He-- I'll make sure that I will do the work, that I will shoot you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, Froiko and Asia had passed, already the-- the road. They were shooting at them. And somebody-- another shoulder-- another soldier brought them to the-- to the headquarters. As we were there, one of the German soldiers that was guarding the high commander said to us, in Polish, he said, are you Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: We said, yes, we are Poles. We are Czechs. And they caught us. We don't know why. Our house is burned, and we have nowhere to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said to us, in Polish, when the commander will come, I'll tell them that you are innocent, that you went out because your house was burning, that you did not cross the road. They suspect that you are going to-- to the Russian side. And I will tell them. And I will try to help you.

speaker: interviewee
text: We could not believe our ears what he said. A minute later, the commander came. He saluted them, the commander. And he said-- so the commander said, I don't want to hear the story. I don't have the time for this story. If they crossed the road-- in German, he told them-- if they crossed the road, then kill them. If not, let them free. He said to them, they did not cross the road. So he said, so let them free. And they let us free. And at night, the Russian army occupied our territory. But Froiko was very injured. A lot-- he lost a lot of blood. And that morning, the following morning, we went to Rowne, to a hospital. He was operated, but he didn't make it. He died, after a few days in Rowne. For us to come to Rowne and see how the city is empty of Jews-- when we walked from the hospital all the way up where we lived, where we went to school, where we had so much fun, and saw the emptiness, the devastating environment, I think that was the hardest thing for us to accept. I cried all the way from the hospital, till we went where-- where we lived in the Jewish quarters. We were very disappointed, very, very disappointed. Then, we found some Jews that were also hidden. And somehow, we had something in-- in common. We asked who survived, and who didn't, and what happened, and what. And we-- we felt that we are part of each other. We could not let go, our feelings. There was still a lot of surviving to do. The fact that my-- that Froiko died was a terrible blow for us, terrible. He suffered so much through the-- the whole war. He went through so much agony-- and we had lived already to see the liberation-- and didn't make it. He was buried in Rowne. But the war was still on. And we were afraid that the Germans will retreat. So we went further down to Russia. We went to Volodymyr-Volynskyl, and settled there. We stayed there for a few weeks, till the Russian army went forward and forward. And they were already in Poland. And then they were already in Germany, so we came back to Rowne. Now, Szloma Goldenberg was a very energetic young man. And he held a very high position in 1939. He was notary public. And that was a very prestigious position in-- in the Soviet Union, because every business deal that has to be done, whether one in real estate, in agriculture, in everything, has to be signed by the-- and sealed by the notary public. He was trusted that position. And when we were liberated again-- we were liberated in 1944, in the-- in February. By the time we came back-- we went to Volodymyr-Volynskyl. By the time we came back, it was, already, May, 1944. He found somebody that-- that knew about his position. And they entrusted him, again, that position.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed in Rowne. And he employed me as a translator. [SIGHS] I stayed with his family, again. And we were married December 19, 1944. Also, their niece, Tania, and one of the boys, [? Motul, ?] were married. It was a double ceremony in the family. It was not a wedding, it was just a ceremony. There was a rabbi in Rowne.

speaker: interviewee
text: The shul was-- was destructed, but it was just the place where we knew that it was the shul. We wanted, somehow, it should have that spiritual feeling, so we got married there. Officially, we did not. Because if the authorities would know that we are married, we would have been separated. The Soviet Union did not allow a husband and wife live together and work together.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then, a new law came out that, whoever was a Polish citizen, till 1939, can repatriate to Poland. And we decided to repatriate. Many people that were citizens came to our office for the notary public to give him-- to give their signature. And here, many people came that were not Polish citizens. But they felt that there is an opportunity to leave the Soviet Union and go into the free world.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, we got in touch with an organization that was called the Berihah. They worked with Israel, together. It was a Zionistic organization that helped Jews leave Russia. It was a very dangerous road, but it started in our office, by giving them the signature. Even many Russian officers from the Russian army came to the office, threw off their uniforms. We gave them old clothes. And they said that they were born in Poland. We gave them the stamp and his signature. And they-- they went-- we found out later that they went to Israel, and they helped to fight in the War of Independence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Of course, they were Jews. But for us, because he did that, the authorities found out, and they arrested him. They arrested Szloma, the notary public. He was my husband already then. And they started to interrogate him and ask him why he did it, and what he did it. He said, he didn't do anything wrong, he did whatever the law permitted him-- just to sign a signature. If somebody came and had a witness that he was this man, he gave them the [INAUDIBLE]. But that was the political party in Russia. And they would have made mince out of him. But at that moment, when they arrested him, we had connection with the judicial department, because our office and their office was together. And we worked together anyway. I ran to the-- to the top of the judicial organization, and I had told them the story. And at that moment, when he was sitting there in the-- in-- in the police station and was integorate-- interrogated, that-- I forgot, already, what was his rank, but a very high-ranking man-- called and told them to leave him free, that he didn't do anything. And he came back. He came back home, and we packed up and went to Poland. We crossed the border to the Bug.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bug River?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Bug River divided Poland and the Soviet Union. And we stayed in Lublin. We stayed in Lublin a few weeks-- a few months. And we made-- again, we got in touch with that organization, the Berihah. It was an illegal organization. They were not functioning legally. They connected the displaced persons with Israel. And they mapped out a road for them, how to come illegally to Palestine. It was under the English occupation then.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- being that we helped them, we worked with them together, we had priority. They told us to go to Bucharest. And from there, a ship from Konstanz will leave, and we will be able to go to Palestine. When we came to Bucharest, that road was found out. And they weren't able, anymore, to go through that road. So they told us to stay in Bucharest, till they'll find out another road how to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, Bucharest was also very touched from the war. There was hunger. We literally didn't have what to eat there, besides the traveling around the Europe. We had no possessions. We had-- it was very, very difficult. But again, we-- we had a lot of strength to be able to overcome all that. After a while, they told us that we should go back through Hungary to Austria. And we stopped in Austria. It was at Rothschild Hospital that they housed all the displaced person. But that was under Russian zone. Austria was divided into four parts. And for us to go to the free world, we had to go to the American zone.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at-- in-- in-- and here, again, in Austria, was without food completely. We really survived on merely nothing. There was no way to get anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian-- the Austrian, the Viennese people were standing by the trains when the trains came. Begged them to give them a piece of bread, give them something to eat. It was very, very hard, at that time. But we stayed in the Rothschild Hospital with other people. Then the people came our-- from the displaced-- from the camps. And they told us all about Auschwitz, about Majdanek. It was mind boggling to find out only young people, teenagers in the-- in the high teens. Hardly-- I don't remember seeing a mother or a father. We were lucky. My-- we survived. My husband had a mother, I had a mother-in-law, and we were a family. We were the only family there. Otherwise, there were only young people in their late teens. They survived the camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after a while, after quite a few weeks that we stayed in Vienna-- and we were constantly in touch with the Berihah-- they told us one evening that they will sail us on the Danube from Vienna to Linz. In Linz, on the other side of the bridge, were the German-- were the American zone.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that-- the one that led us from the Berihah brought us to the place where we went on little boats. They told us, if we have some possessions, we must leave them, because the boats are not sturdy. And it's a few hours ride in those boats. We left whatever we had. We didn't have much. But whatever we left-- we had, we left in Vienna too. And we just, barehanded, came to-- and sailed at night on the Danube and came to the-- to Linz. And I saw that he was bribing the-- the man that was standing on-- on one end of the bridge. And-- and the-- the guard for-- the Russian and the American, on both sides, they let us go. We came to Linz. And then, we settled in Salzburg. They brought us to Salzburg. There was a DP camp. It was called Reidenburg. That was a part of-- of Salzburg. But they were mixed. It was not only for Jews. There were Ukrainians. There were Poles. There were many, many people that were there, displaced persons. The-- the other-- the Jews were all victims from the Nazis. But the-- the others fled because they were afraid in the free world, that they-- they will be in trouble. So they wanted, also, to flee to the free world. And we were mixed in together with everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the meeting the-- in the American zone-- the American soldiers and the officers were wonderful to us. They really felt like we are brothers and sisters. They came with so much compassion. They asked us, what do you want? They-- In the- in the barracks, where we stay, do you want something? They brought chocolate. They-- they brought--

speaker: interviewee
text: We said, what we want is not to be with these people together. We want-- we Jews survived, but they were our enemies, the Ukrainians, the Poles, the Czechs. We want our own quarters.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the German-- the American soldiers intervened and with the American authorities. And we were--
